coredata.dc:title: Electron trapping in magnetic driven graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Pena, Adrian
coredata.dc:description: In this paper we present an in-depth theoretical investigation of an electron scattering process on a graphene quantum dot (GQD) supposed to a perpendicular magnetic field. As it is well known, because of Klein tunneling, an electrostatic potential is not helpful to localize an electron inside a GQD. However, we report here that in the case of a magnetic driven GQD, there emerge scattering resonances characterized by trapped electronic states inside the dot for finite periods of time, otherwise known as quasi-bound states. Using a real space scattering analysis, we highlight in a very intuitive manner how the quasi-bound states are generated and, for a comprehensive investigation, we evaluate their corresponding lifetime (trapping time). As well, we explore the case of an electrostatically biased GQD. We show that this configuration may be very advantageous regarding the control of the trapping times which reach values much higher than in the unbiased case.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Topological insulators
2D materials
Graphene

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dirac fermions in external magnetic field
Scattering analysis of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Sketch of the analyzed system. The circular GQD of radius R is placed on the horizontal
x y −
plane while the magnetic field B is oriented on
z −
direction, at normal incidence on the dot.

Schematic description of the scattering process. The incident electron, represented by the plane wave
ψ
q
i
, is traveling on x direction towards the magnetic driven GQD of radius R , with energy
E =
v
F
ħ q
. After the interaction with the dot, the electron either is reflected and it is represented by
ψ
q
r
or it is transmitted inside the dot and it is represented by
ψ
k
t
.

Scattering efficiency as function of: (a) E and B ; (b) B , for three fixed values of E . In the inset of panel (b) we show the scattering efficiency curve for the case of
E = 20
meV (blue curve), decomposed into components, each of them corresponding to one scattering mode
l = − 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 , 3
.

Scattering analysis of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD, in real space. In each column, the first panel presents the density and, respectively, the second one figures the current, in the near field of the dot. The spatial extension of the GQD is indicated by a black and, respectively, blue circle. Each column of plots is dedicated to one of the scattering regimes indicated by labels from “1” to “4” in Fig. 3(b): (a), (b)
B = 0 . 4
T; (c), (d)
B = 1 . 62
T; (e), (f)
B = 2 . 56
T; (g), (h)
B = 3 . 8
T. (a) The density reveals the signature of diffraction effects. (b) The current confirms the diffraction and no vortex may be observed inside the GQD. (c) The density pattern is quite similar to the previous case, indicating also diffraction effects. (d) The current begins to follow a vortex-like trajectory, indicating the generation of weak quasi-bound states. (e) The density suggests that the dominant effect is no longer the diffraction, but the trapping effects are the noticeable ones. (f) Inside the dot, the current follows an almost enclosed vortex trajectory. (g) The density pattern is typical to a quasi-bound state and the values are one order of magnitude as high, compared to the first three cases. (h) The current demonstrates the genesis of quasi-bound states showing a complete circular vortex inside the GQD.

Resonant
E
r
(empty dots) and, respectively,
E
i
(solid dots) of the energy
E =
E
r
− i
E
i
for a given B . In the inset is displayed the lifetime of each quasi-bound state identified at that given B .

The band structure corresponding to biased (
V > 0
)/non-biased (
V = 0
) regions. The GQD is defined in the biased region (center Dirac cones). The energy of the incident electron, in the unbiased regions, is
E =
v
F
ħ q
. Inside the dot, as the electrostatic potential reduces the Fermi level, the state of the electron lies either in CB or in VB, according to the band index
α =
| E − V |
(
v
F
ħ )
.

Scattering analysis of an electron on a biased magnetic driven GQD. The trapping time (resonant energy in the inset), density and, respectively, current are presented for
l = 0
[panels (a), (b) and (c)] and for
l = 3
[panels (d), (e) and (f)]. In each case, the trapping times were calculated for three values of the applied electrostatic potential, as indicated in the corresponding panel.

head.ce:title: Electron trapping in magnetic driven graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Materials Physics, Atomiştilor 405A, 077125 Măgurele – Ilfov, Romania
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institute of Materials Physics, Atomiştilor 405A, 077125 Măgurele Ilfov, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physics, University of Bucharest, Atomiştilor 405, 077125 Măgurele – Ilfov, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Physics, University of Bucharest

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Physics, University of Bucharest, Atomitilor 405, 077125 Măgurele Ilfov, Romania

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: National Institute of Materials Physics, Atomiştilor 405A, 077125 Măgurele – Ilfov, Romania.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper we present an in-depth theoretical investigation of an electron scattering process on a graphene quantum dot (GQD) supposed to a perpendicular magnetic field. As it is well known, because of Klein tunneling, an electrostatic potential is not helpful to localize an electron inside a GQD. However, we report here that in the case of a magnetic driven GQD, there emerge scattering resonances characterized by trapped electronic states inside the dot for finite periods of time, otherwise known as quasi-bound states. Using a real space scattering analysis, we highlight in a very intuitive manner how the quasi-bound states are generated and, for a comprehensive investigation, we evaluate their corresponding lifetime (trapping time). As well, we explore the case of an electrostatically biased GQD. We show that this configuration may be very advantageous regarding the control of the trapping times which reach values much higher than in the unbiased case.

ce:section.ce:para: Trapping electrons in quantum dots, a very important research subject in the area of condensed matter physics, started some decades ago and nowadays is still receiving much attention from both fundamental and application points of view. Moreover, trapping electrons in a graphene quantum dot (GQD) is even a more interesting topic because, as it is well-known, in the case of an electron which impacts a GQD at normal incidence, the localization inside the dot is forbidden by Klein tunneling [1]. This striking effect is the condensed matter counterpart of the Klein paradox [2], which consists in perfect tunneling of relativistic energies electrons through a potential barrier, regardless of its magnitude.

A number of extensive studies concerning the interaction of graphene with external magnetic fields revealed peculiar effects, for instance Landau quantization [3–7], Hofstadter butterfly spectrum [5,8], quantum Hall effect [9–11] and Aharonov–Bohm effect [12–14]. Furthermore, the linear energy dispersion with zero band gap in the vicinity of Dirac K-points in graphene determines the charged particles to behave like massless Dirac fermions [15]. Consequently, graphene is a system where a relativistic approach must be employed and, actually, this is the reason why a typical relativistic phenomenon such Klein tunneling occurs. Moreover, graphene is an excellent playground for studying interesting phenomena in a quite straightforward way, since all the physics (in massless Dirac fermions approximation) is reduced to massless Dirac equation.

Even though, there is a number of papers, theoretical [16–20] and experimental [21,22], which revealed remarkable results concerning the trapping states of electrons in GQDs, induced by electrostatic fields. Recently, we have demonstrated that a combination of electrostatic bias with twisted light driving is also an excellent configuration for trapping electrons in GQDs [23]. Nevertheless, these electronic states we are discussing about here are not real bound states, but we refer to them as quasi-bound states. Generally speaking, a quasi-bound state is characterized by a positive energy in the continuum and a finite lifetime, usually called trapping time.

In a scattering process of an electron on a GQD, the quasi-bound states arise and their lifetime may be enhanced by appropriately tuning the scattering parameters (energy of the incident electron, GQD radius etc.). Besides the electrostatically confinement of electrons in GQDs, trapping effects were observed in GQDs supposed to a magnetic field [24–27]. All the observations argued that the magnetic field plays a crucial role.

In this paper we are presenting a theoretical model of electron trapping in a magnetic driven GQD with a more physical insight. Our aim is to give a general picture of how the trapping of electrons in such a system becomes achievable. As well, we support our idea by suggestive numerical examples. We also treat the problem of the studied scattering effect on an electrostatically biased magnetic driven GQD and figure out the advantages of this configuration.

The present paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 we present the theory of the studied process. We formulate in Section 2.1 a theoretical model for interaction of Dirac fermions in a GQD with an external magnetic field. Based on this model, in Section 2.2 we describe the scattering process of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD and define the main quantities which characterize the studied phenomenon. In Section 3 we give numerical examples which highlight in a very intuitive manner how the quasi-bound states arise, what are the possibilities to control the scattering process and, as well, how to increase the specific trapping times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dirac fermions in external magnetic field
ce:section.ce:para:
In this subsection we formulate a theoretical model which describes the interaction of a circular GQD of a given radius R with an external magnetic field ( B ). In our approach we consider the dot lying in the horizontal
x y −
plane while the magnetic field is pointing on
z −
direction, perpendicular on the dot. The studied system is schematically depicted in Fig. 1. A slightly different approach is briefly addressed in Ref. [28].

Since the dispersion law in graphene is linear in the vicinity of the Dirac
K −
points with zero band gap, the corresponding low-energy charged particles may be modeled as massless Dirac fermions which move freely with Fermi velocity
v
F
≈ c / 300
with c the speed of light in vacuum [15]. In what follows we restrict ourselves to this approximation.

The state of a Dirac fermion in an external magnetic field is described by the stationary massless Dirac equation (we choose polar coordinates reference system with the origin in the center of the dot):
(1)
H
( r , φ )
ψ
k
( r , φ )
= E
ψ
k
( r , φ )
;
(2)
H
( r , φ )
=
v
F
σ ⋅
[ − i ħ ∇ − e A
( r )
]
.
In the expression of the Hamiltonian (2)
σ =
(
σ
r
,
σ
φ
)
is the Pauli vector whose components are the Pauli matrices, which in polar coordinates read [29]:
(3)
σ
r
=
0
e
− i φ
e
i φ
0
;
σ
φ
=
0
− i
e
− i φ
i
e
i φ
0
.
Moreover, ħ is the reduced Planck constant, e is the elementary charge constant,
A
( r )
= B r / 2
e
φ
is the potential vector in the symmetric gauge with B the strength of the magnetic field and
e
φ
is the unit vector on azimuthal direction. Actually, in this subsection our aim is to derive the physical meaningful solutions of Dirac equation (1).

In what follows we consider the linear energy dispersion
E = α
v
F
ħ k
, where the band index
α = + 1
stands for positive energy states (conduction band, CB) and, respectively,
α = − 1
for negative energy states (valence band, VB). As well, here k is the associated wave number of the Dirac fermion.

Taking into account that the Hamiltonian (2) commutes with the total angular momentum operator
J
z
= − i ħ
∂
φ

J

J

(5)

− ∞

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scattering analysis of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD
ce:section.ce:para: In this subsection we briefly present the scattering process of an electron on a magnetic driven circular GQD of radius R and introduce the main quantities which describe the process.

We consider an incident electron propagating towards the dot on
x −
direction, at normal incidence, with energy
E =
v
F
ħ q
where q is the associated wave number. Therefore the incident electron is described by a plane wave:
(20)
ψ
q
i
( r , φ )
=
1
2
e
i q r cos φ
1
1
=
1
2
∑
l = − ∞
∞
i
l
J
l
( q r )
e
i l φ
i
J
l + 1
( q r )
e
i
( l + 1 )
φ
.
Here we have decomposed the plane wave into a infinite sum of well defined orbital angular momentum states using the well-known Jacobi–Anger expansion,
e
i z cos φ
=
∑
l = − ∞
∞
i
l
J
l
( z )
e
i l φ
. As a result of the collision, the incident electron either is reflected in the outer side of the dot, or it is transmitted inside. We notice here that we deal with an elastic scattering process and in what follows we impose the appropriate conservation laws. The reflected electron wave must obey the infinite boundary conditions specific to such a scattering process, therefore we can decompose it in partial waves as follows [30]:
(21)
ψ
q
r
( r , φ )
=
1
2
∑
l = − ∞
∞
c
l
r
i
l
H
n
( q r )
e
i l φ
i
H
l + 1
( q r )
e
i
( l + 1 )
φ
.
Here
H
l
( x )
is the first kind Hankel function. Indeed, for
x → ∞
the first kind Hankel function behaves as
H
l
( x )
∼
2
π x
e
i
( x −
l π
2
−
π
4
)
, which represents an outgoing wave. Moreover, the Hankel functions of the first kind express also well-defined orbital angular momentum states. Finally, the transmitted electron is described by the following wave function, based on Dirac equation solutions derived in the previous subsection:
(22)
ψ
k
t
( r , φ )
=
∑
l = − ∞
− 1
c
l
t
ψ
k
A −
( r )
e
i l φ
i
ψ

( r )

A +

φ
.
Here k is the associated wave number of the electron inside the GQD. Schematically, the scattering process is depicted in Fig. 2. A device which may be used for experimental investigations of the process in question may be fabricated using well-known methods such as mechanical exfoliation of bulk graphite combined with electron-beam lithography in order to define the desired shape [31–36], etching techniques [37–41] or other chemical synthesis methods [42] and so on.

The scattering coefficients
c
l
r
and
c
l
t
are derived by imposing the following boundary condition:
(23)
ψ
q
i
( R , φ )
+
ψ
q
r
( R , φ )
=
ψ
k
t
( R , φ )
.
After performing some algebra, considering the conservation of the orbital angular momentum, we obtain the following scattering coefficients:
(24)
c
l
t
=
i
2
e
i l π / 2
π k R
[
H
l
( q R )
ψ
k
B ±
( k R )
−
H
l + 1
( q R )
ψ
k
A ±
( k R )
]
;
(25)
c
l
r
=
−
J
l
( q R )
ψ

B ±
( k R )
+
J
l + 1
( q R )
ψ

A ±

H
l
( q R )
ψ
k

( k R )
−

l + 1
( q R )
ψ
k
A ±
( k R )
.

Based on the wave functions (20), (21) and (22) we define now the functions which quantitatively characterize the scattering process. The probability density function is given by
ρ =
ψ
†
ψ
and, respectively, the current by
j =
ψ
†
σ ψ
. Depending on the region where we perform the analysis, we substitute
ψ =
ψ
k
t
( r , φ )
in order to evaluate the density/current inside the dot and, respectively,
ψ =
ψ
q
i
( r , φ )
+
ψ
q
r
( r , φ )
for the outer side of the dot. If we want to perform the analysis only for the reflected electron, we substitute
ψ =
ψ
q
r
( r , φ )
.

If we take into account the asymptotic behavior of the first kind Hankel function, recalled above, the radial component of the far field reflected current reads
(26)
j
r a d
( r , φ )
=
4
π q r
∑
l = − ∞
∞
|
c
l
r
|
2
+
8
π q r
∑
l <
l
′
R e
c
l
r
c
l
′
r
cos
[
( l −
l
′
)
φ ]
.
Following the lines presented in Ref. [43], we introduce the scattering efficiency, which is defined as the scattering cross section divided by the geometric cross section:
(27)
Q =
4
q R
∑
l = − ∞
∞
|
c
l
r
|
2
.

In this section we present the main numerical results obtained based on the theory discussed in the previous section. In what follows we perform a scattering analysis of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD of radius
R = 50
nm
in terms of scattering efficiency ( Q ), density ( ρ ) and current ( j ) in the near field of the dot.

Since the wave functions (20), (21) and (22) are expressed as sums over all possible values of the orbital angular momentum quantum number l , we will consider in that sums only the significant terms. As well, the scattering process may be understood in terms of scattering sub-processes, each of them for a given l , as a result of orbital angular momentum conservation. In what follows each of these individual scattering events will be called the
l
scattering mode.

At the very beginning, let us analyze the scattering efficiency as function of energy of the incident electron ( E ) and the strength of the applied magnetic field
( B )
. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the scattering efficiency exhibits a somewhat oscillatory pattern. We observe here five stripes of high values of scattering efficiency, each of them corresponding to one scattering mode, as indicated by
l = 0 , 1 , 2 , 3 , 4
. These increases of the scattering efficiency are associated with so called scattering resonances, which are characterized by temporarily trapping processes of the electron inside the dot [17,18,44,45]. In what follows we refer to these states of the electron as quasi-bound states, not being real bound states as in the case of an atom, for instance. Which scattering mode is excited when the electron impacts the dot depends on the set of parameters
( E , B )
. Since for a fixed E the scattering efficiency values strongly depend on B , it suggests that the scattering process may be controlled by tuning the intensity of the magnetic field. Taking into account this behavior, we are intended to perform an in-depth investigation of the process in terms of magnetic field intensity.

In Fig. 3(b) we present a detailed characterization of the scattering efficiency as function of B for three values of energy of the incident electron. We start the analysis with the blue curve, which corresponds to
E = 20
meV. In the inset of the plot, we show individually the scattering efficiency curves associated with each scattering mode which composes the total scattering efficiency, for the case of
E = 20
meV. The
l = − 1
mode (black dashed curve) is non-resonantly excited, and its maximum value is the lowest, in comparison with the other modes maxima. In the case of
l = 0
mode (red curve) the scattering is also non-resonant, but with a slightly higher maximum value of the scattering efficiency. The
l = 1
scattering mode (black curve) reveals the signature of a resonance with a broad peak corresponding to
B = 1 . 62
T (see label “2”), therefore we expect the appearance of some trapping effects, even very reduced. In the case of
l = 2
mode (green curve) the scattering resonance is narrower, with a slightly asymmetric typical Lorentz profile with the peak corresponding to
B = 2 . 56
T (see label “3”). This indicates the occurrence of relevant trapping effects. For the
l = 3
scattering mode (purple curve), the resonance is very sharp with the peak centered on
B = 3 . 8
T (see label “4”). As we will stress in what follows and already anticipated here, the narrower is the Lorentzian line shape of the scattering efficiency peak, the more prominent are the trapping effects. In the light of this observation, we expect the most interesting trapping effects for the case of
l = 3
and some opposite effects for the cases of
l = − 1
and
l = 0
.

For
E = 7
meV (red curve), the behavior of the scattering efficiency reveals similarities with the previous case. Also in this case, the superposition of
l = 0
and
l = 1
modes (non-resonantly excited) are visible in the scattering efficiency curve. As well, the
l = 2
mode, resonantly excited, is present with a much higher maximum. On the other hand, the
l = 3
mode is absent.

In the case of
E = 35
meV (green curve), as an overall observation, all the peaks are reduced compared with the two previous cases. As well, inspecting the scattering curve we notice that all the
l = − 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 , 3
modes are excited, as for
E = 20
meV, but all the peaks are broader. In addition, we see that in this case
l = 4
mode is resonantly excited.

So far, assessing the scattering efficiency function in terms of magnetic field intensity, for fixed energies of the incident electron, we conclude that the quasi-bound states arise more prominent in the case of lower energies of the incident electron. As well, as the energy of the incident electron increases, we expect the excitation of higher scattering modes, but with the cost of reduced trapping effects.

In what follows we continue the investigation in terms of real space. We will highlight all the observations presented so far, in a very intuitive manner, analyzing the density and, respectively, current functions in the near field of the GQD. We mention here that this analysis is addressed only to
E = 20
meV case.

In Fig. 4, each column is dedicated to one scattering regime from those indicated with labels from “1” to “4” in Fig. 3(b). The upper panel figures the density in the near field of the dot and the spatial extension of the dot is represented by the black circle. The lower panel shows the density current flow around the GQD region and the border of the dot is figured with the blue circle.

In the case of a magnetic field intensity
B = 0 . 4
T [see label “1” in Fig. 3(b)] the scattering regime is obviously non-resonant and, as can be observed in the inset of the same figure, the only modes which seem to be excited are
l = − 1
and
l = 0
, with a very reduced corresponding scattering efficiency. Thus, we do not expect trapping effects. Indeed, as revealed by the density [see Fig. 4(a)] the electronic wave diffracts on the GQD and the important values can be observed in the outer side. The current, depicted in panel (b), confirms the diffraction effect of the electronic wave on the GQD. We notice that the current forms some branches which avoid the dot region and, as well, we can observe the absence of backscattering. Moreover, on the right-hand side of the dot, in the very vicinity of the border, we can see that the current flows mostly on the x -axis direction. This important amount of current does not originate from some diffraction, but it is a signature of the Klein tunneling, which again reveals the very quenched trapping effects. Furthermore, it is well known in literature that the quasi-bound states are characterized by the occurrence of some vortices inside the dot [46,47]. In our case, assessing the inner region of the GQD, it is clear that no vortex has been generated. Also, even if in panel (a) we can observe high values of density, localized in a very limited area on the lower half of the dot, does not mean that there occurs a trapping event because there is not present any vortex which may trap the incident electron.

The second case we investigate corresponds to
B = 1 . 62
T, where the scattering efficiency curve shows a broad resonance peak, due to the excitation of
l = 1
mode [see label “2” in Fig. 3(b)]. Also in this case, we can note that the scattering process is dominated by diffraction effects [see Fig. 4(c)], but this time the values of the density are a little bit higher in the near field of the dot. The diffraction is again confirmed by the current flow in the outer side of the dot [see panel (d)]. We must be aware that in this case we do not deal only with the weak resonant excitation of
l = 1
mode, but there are non-resonantly excited also
l = − 1
and
l = 0
modes [see inset of Fig. 3(b)], of which specific effects were already discussed. Now we focus our attention on the inner region of the dot. Unfortunately, in the density picture we do not see any concluding pattern which might indicate trapping events. Instead, if we analyze the current, depicted in panel (d), we see clearly that inside the dot the current starts to follow a vortex-like trajectory. This behavior suggests that the quasi-bound states begin to be generated and we can assign this to the excitation of
l = 1
mode, taking into account that we demonstrated previously that the other two modes do not generate quasi-bound states.

The third case is dedicated to
B = 2 . 56
T [see label “3” in Fig. 3(b)]. Recall that in this case, besides the
l = − 1
and
l = 0
, non-resonantly excited, we observed the resonant excitation of
l = 2
mode, with a more prominent Lorentz line shape of the scattering efficiency peak. As well, the width of this peak is obviously much narrower than for
l = 1
mode, previously analyzed. Moreover, as can be seen in the inset of Fig. 3(b), over the
l = − 1
,
l = 0
and
l = 2
curves there is overlapped the tail of the
l = 1
peak. Having in mind this pattern, we expect to retrieve elements of diffraction in the density/current plots. Indeed, in Fig. 4, panels (e) and (f), our anticipation is confirmed. What is important to notice here is that the diffraction is no longer the dominant phenomenon. The density values are higher compared to the first two analyzed cases and localized inside the GQD. The current pattern also explains the generation of the quasi-bound states, by the occurrence of an almost enclosed vortex inside the dot. As well, on the right-hand side of the dot we do not even observe the forward scattering, which means the reduction of the Klein tunneling. This trapping effect is assigned to the resonant excitation of
l = 2
scattering mode.

The last case we analyze is for
B = 3 . 8
T [see label “4” in Fig. 3(b)]. Unlike the previous situation, the resonantly excited mode is
l = 3
, whose corresponding scattering efficiency peak is overlapped with the
l = − 1
,
l = 0
and
l = 1
curves [see inset of Fig. 3(b)]. The Lorentz profile has now even a narrower width than for
l = 2
. In this scattering regime, the trapping effects are the most noticeable and the diffraction is basically suppressed. In Fig. 4(g) we observe the quasi-localization of the incident electron inside the dot. This density pattern is typical to a trapping effect [43] and its values are one order of magnitude as high, in comparison with the first three analyzed cases. In panel (h) we can see how the current encloses on a complete vortex trajectory, which is the fundamental signature of a quasi-localized state. In the near vicinity of the GQD boundary, it is visible how the electron escapes the dot, but that amount of current is very reduced. Moreover, there exist some regions where the electron penetrates again the dot border at non-normal angles, which causes the suppression of the Klein tunneling.

So far, this extensive scattering analysis enables us to draw a mid-term conclusion. The enhancement of trapping effects may be achieved by controlling the scattering regime, by varying the intensity of the magnetic field, in a way such that the diffraction effects to be eluded. As well, the genesis of quasi-bound states is challenged by the manifestation of the Klein tunneling.

Besides the real space analysis, performed in terms of density and current, for a comprehensive investigation of the trapping effects, we must evaluate the lifetime of the quasi-bound states (trapping times). Firstly, let us introduce the method. As we have already seen, the energy of the incident electron takes positive values in the continuum and not negative values as in the case of a real bound state. In order to calculate the trapping times, we must analyze the scattering resonances in terms of complex valued energies of the incident electron
E =
E
r
− i
E
i
, where the real part
E
r
is the resonant energy and
E
i
gives the lifetime ( τ ) of the quasi-bound state by
τ = ħ /
E
i
. Note that
E
i
must take only real values.

We specified at the very beginning that the overall scattering process may be decomposed into individual scattering sub-processes, therefore this time we investigate separately each scattering mode, which has its specific trapping time.

In order to derive the complex energy of the incident electron, we match the transmitted wave function with the reflected one, on the boundary of the dot, by virtue of continuity [18]. Since the magnetic field does not modify the kinetic energy of the incident electron, we consider
q = k
, thus we deal with the following transcendental equation for k :
(28)
ψ
k
A ±
( r )
ψ
k
B ±
( r )
=
H
l
( k r )
H
l + 1
( k r )
r = R
,
for a given mode
l = ⋯
, − 1 , 0 , 1 , 2 , …
. In Eq. (28) we consider
k =
k
r
− i
k
i
and, taking into account the linear dispersion law specific to graphene, the lifetime of the quasi-bound state is given by
τ = 1 /
v
F
k
i
. Each scattering resonance for a given R , l and B may be put in a one-to-one correspondence with a solution of Eq. (28).

For each resonance found by numerically solving Eq. (28), we present in Fig. 5 sets of values (
E
r
,
E
i
) with open and, respectively, solid dots with respect to their corresponding B . The vertical blue lines, which connect the dots, are intended for an easier qualitative estimation of the trapping time at its corresponding
E
r
. In the inset of each panel we figure out the lifetime of each quasi-bound state, for that value of B at which the resonance occurs.

Since for
l = 0
we have seen that the scattering is characterized by a weak quasi-localization and, on the contrary, for
l = 3
we demonstrated noticeable trapping effects, in the trapping times analysis we have considered these two opposite cases. In panel (a) we present the results for
l = 0
and, respectively, in panel (b) for
l = 3
. Having characterized these two scenarios, we can make an overall picture about the studied process.

As a general effect, we observe that the increasing of the magnetic field intensity causes the monotonously rising of the trapping times. In the case of
l = 0
the rising of the trapping times starts to become visible at lower values of B , in contrast to the case of
l = 3
. This observation is in accordance with what we have noticed when investigated the scattering efficiency. An other similitude we want to highlight here is related to the resonance found for
l = 3
at
B = 3 . 75
T and
E
r
= 20 . 4473
meV. Recall that almost the same result was found when studied the scattering efficiency for
E = 20
meV [see Fig. 3(b)].

Even though we have proven that the trapping of electrons in magnetic driven GQDs may be achieved, the trapping times are relatively small. For instance, in the case of
l = 3
where we observed the most pronounced trapping effects, the lifetimes of those quasi-bound states are roughly only three times as high compared to
l = 0
case, when the scattering in non-resonant.

In what follows we will stress that much more interesting is the configuration of a biased magnetic driven GQD. As will be presented, the density and the lifetime of the quasi-bound states may be increased by applying an electrostatic potential
V > 0
on the GQD.

Let us briefly describe the system. The state of the incident electron, which propagate towards the dot with energy
E =
v
F
ħ q
, lies in the CB (
α = + 1
). If the electron penetrates the potential barrier and enters the dot, its state corresponds either to CB or VB (
α = − 1
), depending on the magnitude of V with respect to E . In this case, the corresponding wave number of the electron will be
k =
| E − V |
/
(
v
F
ħ )
and, respectively, the band index
α = s g n
( E − V )
. In Fig. 6 we display the band structure of the studied system.

In Fig. 7 we present the trapping time (resonant energy in the insets), density and, respectively, current for the cases of
l = 0
and
l = 3
scattering modes. For each case we analyze the scattering process for three given values of V . The spatial extension of the GQD is figured with white circle in the density panels and, respectively, with blue circle in the current panels.

The lifetime of the quasi-bound states corresponding to
l = 0
mode seems to be strongly influenced by the applied bias. As we can see in panel (a), for
V = 72
meV (red dots) there exist two values of
E
r
and B which give rise to long lasting quasi-bound states, namely
E
r
= 0 . 011
meV at
B = 0 . 18
T and, respectively,
E = 0 . 009
meV at
B = 3 . 15
T. This behavior of the trapping time with respect to B looks very strange compared to the unbiased case, where the trapping time was rising monotonously with B (recall Fig. 5). Moreover, in the present case, the intensity of the applied magnetic field must be tuned very fine, taking into account that between those two sharp peak values (
τ ≈ 0 . 26
ns
and
τ ≈ 0 . 37
ns
) the trapping times decrease considerably and bias is not useful. In the case of
V = 73
meV the behavior of the trapping time is similar, but the maximum values are more reduced. Actually, in this situation the bias does not contribute essentially to the enhancement of the trapping features of the system. For
V = 75
meV, the first peak does no longer occur and the second one, corresponding to a higher B , indicates an important trapping time
τ = 0 . 3
ns
. By performing this analysis, we demonstrated that the lifetime of the quasi-bound states may be enhanced by applying an electrostatic potential, but with the cost of not being able to generate such effects at any value of the magnetic field intensity. Regardless of those peaks, the values of the trapping times are
τ ≈ 0 . 002
ns
, which are typical to the unbiased configuration. Also, we mention here that all these peculiar phenomena manifest for VB states of the electron. Regarding the resonant energies, we can observe in the inset of panel (a), an unusual pattern. As well, the resonant energies which give rise to that long lasting quasi-bound states we have discussed above (for
V = 72
meV and
V = 75
meV) are the extremities of that hill-like pattern.

Now, we go further and investigate the scattering resonance corresponding to
E
r
= 0 . 011
meV,
B = 0 . 18
T and
V = 72
meV. In panel (b) the density is displayed. In the present scattering regime, the density image [see panel (b)] suggests a very pronounced trapping phenomenon, since those high values of density are concentrated in the center of the dot. Compared to the unbiased configuration [see Fig. 4(a)], in the present regime the density is higher with up to six orders of magnitude. As well, it is obvious that the applied electrostatic potential totally suppresses the diffraction effects.

Not only the density, but also the current shows a peculiar image. We can observe in panel (c) how the current flows in the inner region of the dot, following perfect circular concentric vortex trajectories with alternating rotation senses, which trap the electron inside. We can observe also some small amounts of current which escape the GQD region, flow around, and enter the dot again, giving rise to a large vortex in the inner vicinity of the GQD border.

For the case of
l = 3
scattering mode, the behavior of the trapping times seems to be different, in comparison with the previous analyzed situation. In panel (d) we show the results. The trapping properties of the GQD are more difficult to control, since the scattering effect is very vulnerable to the variation of the applied electrostatic potential magnitude. For instance, we proved this for three close values of the bias. For
V = 74 . 9
meV (red dots), in the case of the resonance identified at
E
r
≈ 0 . 165
meV and
B = 0 . 6
T, the trapping time rises extremely, up to
τ ≈ 730
s, which is an outstanding trapping performance. For the case of
V = 75
meV (blue dots), the trapping time is also high, but about seven times smaller. In the case of
V = 75 . 1
meV (green dots) the bias effect is absent, compared to the previous two cases. Apart these two high values of the trapping time, evaluating the results within panel (d), there seems that the bias does not enhance the trapping features, but the specific lifetimes are from
τ = 1
ns
to
τ = 1
0
6
ns
. This is again a striking effect, taking into account that in the unbiased case the specific lifetime of the quasi-bound states was
τ ∼ 1
0
− 2
ns
[see Fig. 5(b)].
If the values of the trapping times are so sensitive to the variation of the bias strength, the resonant energies do not vary noticeably, as can be observed in the inset of panel (d).

In what follows we investigate the resonance corresponding to the case of
E ≈ 0 . 165
meV,
B = 0 . 6
T and
V = 74 . 9
meV, in terms of density and current. The density [panel (e)] indicates that the applied electrostatic potential generates quasi-bound states. Even though in this case the highest values of the density are no longer encountered in the center of the dot, it does not mean that the trapping is depleted. On the contrary, those values of density, up to ten order of magnitude higher than for
l = 0
[see panel (b)] and fifteen orders higher than in the unbiased case [see Fig. 5(g)], demonstrate that the
l = 3
mode is characterized by the most interesting scattering effects.

The current [panel (f)] exhibits also a very expressive pattern. With the highest values, the current generates a big vortex inside the GQD, which gives rise to the discussed boosted quasi-bound state. This vortex encloses in a perfect circular trajectory with only one rotation sense. On the other hand, a circular current, rotating in the opposite sense, may be observed outside, but the values are insignificant. Moreover, the diffraction is absent and the Klein tunneling seems to be basically completely damped.

To sum up, in this paper we have presented an in-depth theoretical analysis of the scattering process of an electron on a magnetic driven GQD. In this respect, firstly we have elaborated a model for interaction of Dirac fermions, within a circular GQD, with an external magnetic field. Further, we have generally described the scattering phenomenon and introduced the main quantities used to characterize the system. Based on this first part of theory, we have performed a numerical investigation in order to intuitively emphasize how an incident electron at a normal angle on a magnetic driven GQD may be trapped.

By analyzing the density plots, we have seen that the non-resonant scattering is governed by diffraction effects of the electronic incident wave on the GQD. As well, the current shows either a trajectory which avoids the dot (which confirms the diffraction), or straightly crosses the dot as a signature of Klein tunneling. On the other hand, the resonant scattering is governed by quasi-localization of the incident electron and a damped or absent diffraction. The generation of quasi-bound states may be observed in the density patterns where the diffraction fringes are absent and the important values are localized inside the GQD on approximately ring-shaped ranges. The trapping of the electron may be intuitively understood in terms of current, which follows vortex-like trajectories inside the dot. Moreover, in the resonant regime we noticed a considerable reduction of Klein tunneling.

As well, we performed an investigation regarding the trapping times which showed the specific lifetime scales corresponding to non-resonantly and, respectively, resonantly excited scattering modes. Also, we found that the lifetime of quasi-bound states monotonously increases with the rising of the magnetic field intensity.

Finally, we explored the biased configuration. The main result consists in the possibility to enhance the trapping times by applying an electrostatic potential, reaching values much higher than in the unbiased case. However, in this case there emerges an interplay between the magnitude of the bias and the intensity of the magnetic field, therefore, in order to gain the maximum effect, these parameters must be finely adjusted.

In conclusion, in this paper we reported on the possibility to trap electrons in a GQD by using a magnetic field. We found that the trapping effects are challenged by diffraction of the incident electron wave on the GQD and the manifestation of the Klein tunneling. Therefore, the generation of long lasting quasi-bound states may be controlled by adjusting the scattering parameters in a way that those two processes to be suppressed. We also took advantage of a real space scattering analysis and emphasized in terms of density and current how the incident electron becomes trapped inside the GQD, in both unbiased/biased cases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral tunnelling and the Klein paradox in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. I. Katsnelson, K. S. Novoselov, and A. K. Geim. Chiral tunnelling and the Klein paradox in graphene. Nature Physics, 2(9):620–625, September 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Seventy years of the Klein paradox

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Dombey and A. Calogeracos. Seventy years of the Klein paradox. Physics Reports, 315(1):41–58, 1999.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic states and Landau levels in graphene stacks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Guinea, A. H. Castro Neto, and N. M. R. Peres. Electronic states and Landau levels in graphene stacks. Phys. Rev. B, 73:245426, Jun 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel electric field effects on Landau levels in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Vinu Lukose, R. Shankar, and G. Baskaran. Novel electric field effects on Landau levels in graphene. Phys. Rev. Lett., 98:116802, Mar 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning of energy levels and optical properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Z. Zhang, Kai Chang, and F. M. Peeters. Tuning of energy levels and optical properties of graphene quantum dots. Phys. Rev. B, 77:235411, Jun 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Landau quantization in graphene monolayer, Bernal bilayer, and Bernal trilayer on graphite surface

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Long-Jing Yin, Si-Yu Li, Jia-Bin Qiao, Jia-Cai Nie, and Lin He. Landau quantization in graphene monolayer, bernal bilayer, and bernal trilayer on graphite surface. Phys. Rev. B, 91:115405, Mar 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafast carrier dynamics in Landau-quantized graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Florian Wendler, Andreas Knorr, and Ermin Malic. Ultrafast carrier dynamics in Landau-quantized graphene. Nanophotonics, 4(3):224–249, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Genesis of the Floquet Hofstadter butterfly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. H. Kooi, A. Quelle, W. Beugeling, and C. Morais Smith. Genesis of the Floquet Hofstadter butterfly. Phys. Rev. B, 98:115124, Sep 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental observation of the quantum Hall effect and Berry’s phase in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuanbo Zhang, Yan-Wen Tan, Horst L. Stormer, and Philip Kim. Experimental observation of the quantum Hall effect and Berry’s phase in graphene. Nature, 438(7065):201–204, 2005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room-temperature quantum Hall effect in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. S. Novoselov, Z. Jiang, Y. Zhang, S. V. Morozov, H. L. Stormer, U. Zeitler, J. C. Maan, G. S. Boebinger, P. Kim, and A. K. Geim. Room-temperature quantum Hall effect in graphene. Science, 315(5817):1379–1379, 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrical manipulation of edge states in graphene and the effect on quantum hall transport

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Ostahie, M. Niţă, and A. Aldea. Electrical manipulation of edge states in graphene and the effect on quantum Hall transport. Phys. Rev. B, 91:155409, Apr 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aharonov-Bohm effect and broken valley degeneracy in graphene rings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Recher, B. Trauzettel, A. Rycerz, Ya. M. Blanter, C. W. J. Beenakker, and A. F. Morpurgo. Aharonov-Bohm effect and broken valley degeneracy in graphene rings. Phys. Rev. B, 76:235404, Dec 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interplay of Aharonov-Bohm and Berry phases in gate-defined graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Julia Heinl, Martin Schneider, and Piet W. Brouwer. Interplay of Aharonov-Bohm and Berry phases in gate-defined graphene quantum dots. Phys. Rev. B, 87:245426, Jun 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle:
Aharonov-Bohm conductance oscillations and current equilibration in local
n − p
junctions in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Żebrowski, A. Mreńca-Kolasińska, and B. Szafran. Aharonov-Bohm conductance oscillations and current equilibration in local n-p junctions in graphene. Phys. Rev. B, 98:155420, Oct 2018.
A. H. Castro Neto, F. Guinea, N. M. R. Peres, K. S. Novoselov, and A. K. Geim. The electronic properties of graphene. Rev. Mod. Phys., 81:109–162, Jan 2009.
P. G. Silvestrov and K. B. Efetov. Quantum dots in graphene. Phys. Rev. Lett., 98:016802, Jan 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fock-darwin states of Dirac electrons in graphene-based artificial atoms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hong-Yi Chen, Vadim Apalkov, and Tapash Chakraborty. Fock-Darwin states of Dirac electrons in graphene-based artificial atoms. Phys. Rev. Lett., 98:186803, May 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron localization in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Prabath Hewageegana and Vadym Apalkov. Electron localization in graphene quantum dots. Phys. Rev. B, 77:245426, Jun 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron dynamics in graphene with gate-defined quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Pieper, R. L. Heinisch, and H. Fehske. Electron dynamics in graphene with gate-defined quantum dots. EPL (Europhysics Letters), 104(4):47010, nov 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron confinement in graphene with gate-defined quantum dots
Phys. Status Solidi B Basic Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Holger Fehske, Georg Hager, and Andreas Pieper. Electron confinement in graphene with gate-defined quantum dots. Physica Status Solidi B Basic Research, 252(8):1868–1871, August 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Imaging electrostatically confined Dirac fermions in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Juwon Lee, Dillon Wong, Jr. Velasco, Jairo, Joaquin F. Rodriguez-Nieva, Salman Kahn, Hsin-Zon Tsai, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Alex Zettl, Feng Wang, Leonid S. Levitov, and Michael F. Crommie. Imaging electrostatically confined Dirac fermions in graphene quantum dots. Nature Physics, 12(11):1032–1036, November 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Klein tunnelling and electron trapping in nanometre-scale graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Christopher Gutiérrez, Lola Brown, Cheol-Joo Kim, Jiwoong Park, and Abhay N. Pasupathy. Klein tunnelling and electron trapping in nanometre-scale graphene quantum dots. Nature Physics, 12(11):1069–1075, November 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electron trapping in twisted light driven graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adrian Pena. Electron trapping in twisted light driven graphene quantum dots. Phys. Rev. B, 105:045405, Jan 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic confinement of massless Dirac Fermions in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. De Martino, L. Dell’Anna, and R. Egger. Magnetic confinement of massless Dirac fermions in graphene. Phys. Rev. Lett., 98:066802, Feb 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bound states of Dirac electrons in a graphene-based magnetic quantum dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dali Wang and Guojun Jin. Bound states of Dirac electrons in a graphene-based magnetic quantum dot. Physics Letters A, 373(44):4082–4085, 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quasibound states of Schrödinger and Dirac electrons in a magnetic quantum dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ramezani Masir, A. Matulis, and F. M. Peeters. Quasibound states of Schrödinger and Dirac electrons in a magnetic quantum dot. Phys. Rev. B, 79:155451, Apr 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quasi-bound states in an NPN-type nanometer-scale graphene quantum dot under a magnetic field

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yueting Pan, Haijiao Ji, Xin-Qi Li, and Haiwen Liu. Quasi-bound states in an npn-type nanometer-scale graphene quantum dot under a magnetic field. Scientific Reports, 10(1), 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytic model of the energy spectrum of a graphene quantum dot in a perpendicular magnetic field

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Schnez, K. Ensslin, M. Sigrist, and T. Ihn. Analytic model of the energy spectrum of a graphene quantum dot in a perpendicular magnetic field. Phys. Rev. B, 78:195427, Nov 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The cylindrical δ -potential and the Dirac equation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M Loewe, F Marquez, and R Zamora. The cylindrical δ-potential and the Dirac equation. Journal of Physics A: Mathematical and Theoretical, 45(46):465303, oct 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle:
Caustics due to a negative refractive index in circular graphene
p − n
junctions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observation of excited states in a graphene quantum dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Schnez, F. Molitor, C. Stampfer, J. Güttinger, I Shorubalko, T. Ihn, and K. Ensslin. Observation of excited states in a graphene quantum dot. Applied Physics Letters, 94(1):012107, 2009.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hanjun Sun, Li Wu, Weili Wei, and Xiaogang Qu. Recent advances in graphene quantum dots for sensing. Materials Today, 16(11):433–442, 2013.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Schulz, R. L. Heinisch, and H. Fehske. Electron flow in circular graphene quantum dots. Quantum Matter, 4(4):346–351, 2015.

Nano Letters, 16(9):5798–5805, July 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Scattering of two-dimensional Dirac fermions on gate-defined oscillating quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Schulz, R. L. Heinisch, and H. Fehske. Scattering of two-dimensional Dirac fermions on gate-defined oscillating quantum dots. Phys. Rev. B, 91:045130, Jan 2015.
